task: Create a SQL query to find the top 10 customers by lifetime value excluding refunds, from a schema you define

criteria.description: Schema is realistic, normalized, and supports the query requirements
criteria.pass_condition: Schema must include: (1) Proper 3NF normalization with customers, orders, order_items, and separate transactions table, (2) Realistic data types (e.g., DECIMAL for amounts, TIMESTAMP for dates), (3) Proper constraints (NOT NULL, CHECK constraints for positive amounts), (4) Foreign key relationships explicitly defined, (5) Refunds as separate records with clear relationship to original transactions, (6) At least 5 realistic columns per table with business-appropriate naming.

sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if 3NF, 0.5 if some denormalization, 0.0 if flat

sub_attributes.description: Refunds modeled as distinct records, not negative amounts
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if refund table or refund_type flag, 0.5 if negative amounts, 0.0 if not modeled
% of tables with appropriate types and constraints

pass_examples: customers, orders, order_items, payments (with type: 'charge'|'refund') + indexes
fail_examples: Single 'transactions' table with all data flattened

criteria.description: Query returns correct results — top 10 by LTV excluding refunds
criteria.pass_condition: Correctly aggregates payments minus refunds per customer. Uses proper GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 10. Handles NULL edge cases.

sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if correct LTV calc, 0.5 if close, 0.0 if wrong

sub_attributes.description: Proper GROUP BY customer with ORDER BY + LIMIT
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if correct, 0.0 if missing or wrong

sub_attributes.description: COALESCE or equivalent for customers with no refunds
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if handles nulls, 0.5 if partially, 0.0 if would fail on nulls

pass_examples: COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN type='charge' THEN amount END), 0) - COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN type='refund' THEN amount END), 0)
fail_examples: SELECT * FROM customers ORDER BY amount — no aggregation

criteria.description: Query is well-formatted, commented, and uses CTEs appropriately
criteria.pass_condition: Uses CTEs for complex subqueries. Consistent formatting. Meaningful aliases. Comments on non-obvious logic.

sub_attributes.description: Uses CTEs for readability instead of nested subqueries
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if CTEs for distinct logical steps, 0.5 if inline subqueries, 0.0 if spaghetti
1.0 if clean formatting, 0.5 if mostly ok, 0.0 if unformatted

sub_attributes.description: Comments on key logic, meaningful aliases
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if commented + good aliases, 0.5 if one, 0.0 if neither

pass_examples: WITH customer_charges AS (...), customer_refunds AS (...) SELECT ...
fail_examples: One-liner with nested subqueries and single-letter aliases

criteria.pass_condition: Avoids SELECT *. Uses appropriate JOINs instead of correlated subqueries. Includes comment suggesting relevant indexes (e.g., on customer_id, transaction_date). No obvious anti-patterns like DISTINCT without GROUP BY.
pass_examples: Index on payments(customer_id, type, amount), avoids correlated subqueries
fail_examples: SELECT * with correlated subquery per customer row
